---
date: "2026-06-22"
---

## The Speak-From Deck — Furnace and Three Enemies

- Part 5 — speak-from deck: furnace and three enemies

::: notes
Welcome back to how I pick and kill a project, part five of nine. In part four we worked the three warm-up questions. Now we open the speak-from deck — the scaffold I actually talk over in the room — and walk its first two slides: the funnel as furnace, and the three enemies of cheap killing gathered into one frame.
:::

## Slide One: The Funnel Is a Furnace

- Many ideas in the top, **one project** out
- Its job is to **destroy**, not select
- Burns about **ten ideas** per survivor
- Finishers kill what they love, **without grief**
- Today: not how to pick, but how to **kill cheaply**

::: notes
Slide one restates the spine of the whole session. The research process is a funnel: wide at the top with many ideas, one project out the bottom. Its real job is not to select but to destroy, burning roughly ten ideas per survivor. The skill that separates finishers from non-finishers is not generating ideas — everyone can — it is killing the ones you already love, early and without grief. I abandon ten to thirty ideas to get one paper to a journal; that is not failure, that is the process working. Today is the unglamorous half of yesterday's chapter: not how to pick, but how to kill, cheaply, on paper, before the data and the deadline make it expensive.
:::

## Slide Two: Three Enemies of Cheap Killing

- **Sunk cost** — spent time argues for itself; ignore it
- **The inert project** — answering a question **nobody asked**
- **The fatal flaw** — a crack that means **stop now**
- Not pessimism — running the **furnace hot**
- So only survivors **see daylight**

::: notes
Slide two gathers the three enemies into one frame. Sunk cost: the week you already spent cleaning data argues for its own survival, but it should not — the only honest question ignores what is spent and asks, from here, is this still the best use of the weeks I have left? The inert project: flawlessly answering a question nobody asked, caught by the so-what test pointed at your own project weekly. And the fatal flaw: not a weakness you patch, but a crack that means stop now. The discipline here is not pessimism — it is running the furnace hot enough that only the survivors see daylight.
:::

## Key Takeaway

::: callout-tip
## Key takeaway
The deck's spine: the funnel is a **furnace** that destroys about ten ideas per survivor, and three enemies make killing expensive when ignored — **sunk cost**, **the inert project**, and **the fatal flaw**. Run the furnace hot so only survivors see daylight.
:::

::: notes
To summarize part five: slide one restates the funnel as a furnace whose job is to destroy roughly ten ideas per survivor, and whose true skill is killing what you love without grief. Slide two gathers the three enemies — sunk cost, the inert project, and the fatal flaw — and frames the discipline not as pessimism but as running the furnace hot so only survivors see daylight. In part six we put the fatal-flaw checklist on its own slide and meet two of my own projects as live funnels.
:::
